pages.title: Statement of theorems
pages.html:
1 Statement of theorems

In all this talk, \(S\) will be a noetherian scheme and \(A\) a noetherian ring on the left, of torsion cancelled by an invertible integer on \(S\). Our main result is the following.

Theorem 1.1

We assume \(S\) regular dimension \(0\) or \(1\). Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) a morphism of \(S\)-schemes of finite type and \(\mathscr{F}\) a constructible sheaf of \(A\)-modules left on \(X\). Then the sheaves \(\mathsf{R}^i f_\ast \mathscr{F}\) are constructible.

The evidence will be given in the paragraph 2 and in 3.10.

1.2 Note

In characteristic \(0\), this result is less general than [2], which proves the conclusion of the theorem for any finite type morphism of excellent patterns of characteristic \(0\). [2] On the one hand uses the resolution of singularities, on the other hand the diagrams are of equal characteristic (to be able to deduce from the resolution the "theorem of purity").

1.3 Note

We will say that a complex \(K\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}(X,A)\) is constructible if its cohomology sheaves are constructible and we will note with an index \(c\) the subcategory of \(\mathsf{D}(X,A)\) (or \(\mathsf{D}^+\), \(\mathsf{D}^-\), \(\mathsf{D}^b\)) formed of constructible complexes. In the language of derived categories, which we will use freely, 1.1 says \(\mathsf{R} f_\ast:\mathsf{D}^+(X,A) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{D}^+(Y,A)\) sends \(\mathsf{D}_c^+\) to \(\mathsf{D}_c^+\).

  1. For \(K\) reduced to a sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) in degree \(0\), we have \(\mathscr{H}^i\mathsf{R} f_\ast K = \mathsf{R}^i f_\ast \mathscr{F}\); the derived statement therefore implies the theorem.

  2. In the other direction, the spectral sequence is invoked.

    \[ E_1^{p q} = \mathsf{R}^q f_\ast \mathscr{H}^p(K) \Rightarrow \mathscr{H}^{p+q} \mathsf{R} f_\ast K \text{.} \]

Talking about the language of the derived categories to the advantage of replacing with a simple transitivity formula \(\mathsf{R}(f g)_\ast = \mathsf{R} f_\ast \mathsf{R} g_\ast\) a spectral sequence of Leray \(E_2^{p q} = \mathsf{R}^p f_\ast \mathsf{R}^q g_\ast \Rightarrow \mathsf{R}^{p+q} (f g)_\ast\).

Under the assumptions of 1.1, it follows from [2] that \(\mathsf{R} f_\ast\) is of finished cohomological dimension. This allows above to replace \(\mathsf{D}^+\) by \(\mathsf{D}\), \(\mathsf{D}^-\) or \(\mathsf{D}^b\).

1.4 The ideas of the demonstration

  1. Poincaré's duality allows us to treat the case where \(X\) is smooth, \(\mathscr{F}\) locally constant, and \(Y=S\). The dimension hypothesis appears by calculating \(\mathsf{R}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\) on \(S\).

  2. On factorize \(f\) en \(g j\): \(g\) clean and \(j\) open dive. We have \(\mathsf{R} f_\ast = \mathsf{R} g_\ast \mathsf{R} j_\ast\) and the finiteness theorem for own morphisms control \(\mathsf{R} g_\ast\). Desiring, we come to assume \(x\) smooth, \(\mathscr{F}\) locally constant, \(f\) an open dive and \(Y\) clean on \(S\).

  3. A recurrence on \(\dim X\) (with change of \(S\)) allows, roughly modo, to assume the true theorem outside a part of \(Y\) finished on \(S\). Noting \(a:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) and \(b:Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) structural morphisms, it is then shown that if the theorem was false for \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathsf{R} f_\ast\), it would also be so for \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathsf{R} b_\ast \mathsf{R} f_\ast = \mathsf{R} a_\ast\): contradiction.

Corollary 1.5

Under the assumptions of the theorem, the categories \(\mathsf{D}_c(X,A)\) and \(\mathsf{D}_c(Y,A)\) are transformed into each other by \(4\) operations \(\mathsf{R} f_\ast\), \(\mathsf{R} f_!\), \(f^\ast\), \(\mathsf{R} f^!\).

\(\mathsf{R} f_\ast\) is treated above, \(\mathsf{R} f_!\) in [2], \(f^\ast\) is clear. Remains \(\mathsf{R} f^!\). The problem is local, which suggests that \(f\) is factored into \(X\xrightarrow i Z \xrightarrow g Y\) (\(i\) closed diving and \(g\) smooth, purely relative dimension \(n\)). The duality of Poincaré \(\mathsf{R} g_! K(n)[2 n]\) and transitivity \(\mathsf{R} f^! = \mathsf{R} i^! \mathsf{R} g^!\) bring us back to prove 1.5 for \(i\). For all \(L\in \mathsf{D}(Z,A)\), if \(j\) is the inclusion in \(Z\) of \(U=Z\setminus X\), \(i_\ast \mathsf{R} i^! L\) is the mapping cylinder of \(K\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R} j_\ast j^\ast K\) and 1.5 for \(i\) results from 1.1 for \(j\).

Corollary 1.6

Either \(X\) as in the theorem, and suppose \(A\) commutative. So if \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{G}\) are constructible sheaves of \(A\)-modules on \(X\), the \(\operatorname{Ext}_A^i(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) are constructible: \(\mathsf{R}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\) sends \(\mathsf{D}_c^-(X,A)\times \mathsf{D}_c^+(X,A)\) in \(\mathsf{D}_c^+(X,A)\).

By unscrewing \(\mathscr{F}\), one boils down to assume \(\mathscr{F}\) of the form \(j_!\mathscr{F}_1\) (\(j:Y\hookrightarrow X\)) a locally closed dive and \(\mathscr{F}_1\) locally constant on \(Y\).

\[ \mathsf{R}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(j_! \mathscr{F}_1,\mathscr{G}) = \mathsf{R} j_\ast \operatorname{Hom}(\mathscr{F}_1,\mathsf{R} j^! \mathscr{G}) \text{:} \]

Based on 1.3, we are returned to the case where \(\mathscr{F}\) is locally constant – if not constant since the problem is local. \(\mathscr{F}\) locally constant constructible, we have \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})_x=\hom(\mathscr{F}_x,\mathscr{G}_x)\), and likewise for \(\mathsf{R}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\). For \(\mathscr{F}\) constant, we can therefore calculate \(\operatorname{Ext}^i\) using a finite projective resolution of its constant value, and \(\operatorname{Ext}^i\) are constructible if \(\mathscr{G}\) is – hence the corollary.

1.7 Note

Since \(\mathsf{R} f_\ast\) is of finished cohomological dimension, it transforms complexes of finished tor-dimensional (rep. \(\leqslant d\)) in finished tor-dimensions complexes (resp. \(\leqslant d\)) [2]. [2] and proof of 1.5 then show that the finite tor-dimension is stable by \(4\) operations \(\mathsf{R} f_\ast\), \(\mathsf{R} f_!\), \(f^\ast\), \(\mathsf{R} f^!\). 1.6 (destruct \(K\) according to a partition of \(x\) to assume locally constant cohomology sheaves, then locate to replace \(K\) with a finite complex of locally free sheaves of the finite type) shows then that, for \(A\) commutative, \(\mathsf{R}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\) induced

\[ \mathsf{R}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}:\mathsf{D}_\text{ctf}^b(X,A)\times \mathsf{D}_\text{tf}^b(X,A) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{D}_\text{tf}^+(X,A) \]

(t.f.: finished tor-dimension).

1.8

Under the assumptions of the theorem, the same method allows us to prove a local biduality theorem \(K\xrightarrow{\sim} D D K\) (paragraph 4) We also prove a theorem of finiteness for the bundles of evanescent cycles. For \(S\) any one still obtains a "generic" theorem:

Theorem 1.9

Either \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) a morphism of \(S\)-schedemas of finite type and \(\mathscr{F}\) a constructible sheaf of \(A\)-modules on \(X\). There is a dense open \(U\) of \(S\) such as

  1. Above \(U\), \(\mathsf{R}^i f_\ast \mathscr{F}\) are constructible, and null except a finite number of them.

  2. The formation of \(\mathsf{R}^i f_\ast \mathscr{F}\) is compatible with any basic change \(S' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\subset S\).

For \(S\) the spectrum of a field, we have \(U=S\).

If \(S\) is the spectrum of a field, an argument of passage to the limit provides compatibility to \(S\)-base changes of \(\mathsf{R}^i f_\ast\) for any quasi-compact morphism almost separated from \(S\)-schemes and any sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\).

Corollary 1.10

Be \(x\) a pattern of finite type on \(k\) separably closed and \(\mathscr{F}\) a constructible sheaf of \(A\)-modules. Then the \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F})\) are finite type.

This is the special case \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(k)\), \(Y=S\).

Corollary 1.11

Be \(X\) and \(Y\) two drawings of finite type on \(k\) separably closed and \(K\in \operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}^-(X,A^\circ)\), \(L\in \operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}^-(Y,A)\) of the cluster complexes of modules to the right and left.

\[ \mathsf{R} \Gamma(X,K) \overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathsf{R} \Gamma(Y,L) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X\times Y,\mathrm{pr}_1^\ast K \overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathrm{pr}_2^\ast L) \]

is an isomorphism.

We're going like this. [2]:

\[ \xymatrix{ X\times Y \ar[r]^-{\text{pr}_1} \ar[d]^-{\text{pr}_2} & X \ar[d]^-a \\ Y \ar[r]^-b & \operatorname{Spec}(k) } \]

\(b\), we find that \(\mathsf{R}\mathrm{pr}_{2,\ast}{} \mathrm{pr}_1^\ast K\) is \(b^\ast \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X,K)\). We have then (cf. [2] and proof)

\begin{align*} \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X\times Y,\mathrm{pr}_1^\ast K\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathrm{pr}_2^\ast L) &= \mathsf{R}\Gamma(Y,\mathsf{R} \mathrm{pr}_{2,\ast}(\mathrm{pr}_1^\ast K\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathrm{pr}_2^\ast L)) \\ &= \mathsf{R}\Gamma(Y,(\mathsf{R}\mathrm{pr}_{2,\ast}\mathrm{pr}_1^\ast K)\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} L) \\ &= \mathsf{R}\Gamma(Y,b^\ast \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X,K)\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} L) \\ &= \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X,K)\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathsf{R}\Gamma(Y,L) \end{align*}

1.12

Finally, as a local counterpart of 1.9(ii), we will prove a generic local acyclicity theorem (2.13).

pages.title: Generic theorems
pages.html:
2 Generic Theorems

In this paragraph, we prove 1.9 and generic local acyclic theorem 2.13. To prove 1.9\(S\) integrates. Either \(\eta\) its generic point.

2.1

We'll start by proving 1.9 under the following additional assumptions: \(X\) is smooth on \(S\), purely of relative dimension \(n\), \(A=\mathbb{Z}/m\), \(\mathscr{F}\) is locally constant and \(Y=S\). Either \(\mathscr{F}'=\operatorname{Hom}(\mathscr{F},\mathbb{Z}/m)\). We will show that if the \(\mathsf{R}^i f_! \mathscr{F}'\) are locally constant, the conclusions of 1.9 are valid for \(U=S\). Recall that if \(\mathscr{L}\) is a locally constant sheaf constructible, the \(\operatorname{Ext}^i(\mathscr{L},\mathscr{G})\) are calculated by fiber, and are constructible if \(\mathscr{G}\) is. Also remember that \(\mathbb{Z}/m\) is an \(\mathbb{Z}/m\)-injective module, and is the dualizing module, so that \(\mathscr{F}=\mathsf{R}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathscr{F}',\mathbb{Z}/m)\). The duality of Poincare

\[ \mathsf{R} f_\ast \mathsf{R}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(K,\mathsf{R} f^! L) = \mathsf{R}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathsf{R} f_! K,L) \text{,} \]

for \(K=\mathscr{F}'\), \(L=\mathbb{Z}/m\), \(\mathsf{R} f^! L = \mathbb{Z}/m[2n](n)\), therefore provides

\[ \mathsf{R}^{2n-i} f_\ast \mathscr{F} = \operatorname{Hom}(\mathsf{R}^i f_! \mathscr{F}',\mathbb{Z}/m)(-n) \text{.} \]

The sheaf to the second member is locally constant, of formation compatible with any basic change – hence the assertion.

2.2

Prove 1.9 under the additional assumptions: \(X\) is smooth on \(S\), \(\mathscr{F}\) is locally constant and \(Y=S\).

  1. Decomposing \(X\) in related components, it is assumed that it is purely of a relative dimension \(n\) on \(S\).

  2. Decomposing \(A\) as a product, it is assumed that \(\ell^m A=0\), with \(\ell\) first invertible on \(S\); then we filter \(\mathscr{F}\) by \(\ell^k \mathscr{F}\) to return, subsequently, to the corresponding spectral, in case \(\ell\mathscr{F}=0\). We can then replace \(A\) by \(A/\ell A\) and assume that \(\ell A=0\).

  3. Replacing \(S\) by \(U\) suitable, it can be assumed that there is a galo-finite, spreading coating \(X_1/X\), of Galois group \(G\), such as the reciprocal image \(\mathscr{F}_1\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X_1\) is a constant sheaf, of constant value \(F\). Noting \(f_1\) the projection of \(X_1\) on \(S\), we have then

    \[ \mathsf{R}^i {f_1}_\ast \mathscr{F}_1 = (\mathsf{R}^i {f_1}_\ast \mathbb{Z}/\ell)\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}/\ell} F \text{.} \]

    There is also the spectral sequence of Hochschild-Serre (or: from Leray for the recovery \(X_1/X\))

    \[ \underline{\underline{\operatorname{H}}}^p(G,\mathsf{R}^q {f_1}_\ast \mathscr{F}_1) \Rightarrow \mathsf{R}^{p+q} f_\ast \mathscr{F} \text{.} \]

    Based on 2.1, it can be assumed, even if to shrink \(U\), that \(\mathsf{R}^i {f_1}_\ast \mathbb{Z}/\ell\) is locally constant, of formation compatible with any basic change. The same property then applies to \(\mathsf{R}^i f_\ast \mathscr{F}\). Finally, if \(\overline{\eta}\) is a geometric point located at the generic point \(\eta\) of \(S\), the \(\mathsf{R}^i f_\ast\mathscr{F}\) are almost all null, because the \((\mathsf{R}^i f_\ast \mathscr{F})_{\overline{\eta}} = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X_{\overline{\eta}},\mathscr{F})\) are.

2.3

Prove by recurrence on \(n\) that

\[ \tag{*n} \begin{array}{c} \text{The 1.9 conclusions are true when } \dim{X_\eta}\leqslant n \\ \text{and that } f \text{ is an open dive of dense image.} \end{array} \]

For \(n=0\), even if shrinking \(S\), we have \(X=Y\): \((\ast_0)\) is obvious. Suppose (\(\ast_{n-1})\), and prove (\(\ast _n\)) In (\(\ast_{n-1}\)), "open dive" can be replaced by "dive" as can be seen by factoring in open dive and closed dive.

Lemma 2.4

Quit to shrink \(S\), the conclusions of 1.9 value above \(Y'\subset Y\), the complement \(Y_1\) of \(Y'\) being finished on \(S\).

The assertion is local on \(Y\), that one can assume affine: \(Y\subset \mathbb{A}_S^n\). The hypothesis of recurrence (\(\ast_{n-1}\)) applies to

\[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-f \ar[dr] & Y \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{pr}_i} \\ & \mathbb{A}_S^1 } \]

It therefore exists for each \(i\) dense open \(U_i\) of \(\mathbb{A}_S^1\) such as the conclusions of 1.9 are above \(\mathrm{pr}_i^{-1}(U_i)\); it is above the meeting of \(\mathrm{pr}_i^{-1}(U_i)\), and 2.4 As a result.

2.6

Prove (\(\ast _n\)) for \(X\) smooth on \(S\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) locally constant on \(X\). The problem being local on \(Y\), one can assume \(Y\) affine, then projective (replace \(Y\) by its adhesion in a projective space). Then the formation of \(\mathsf{R} {f_{(x)}}_\ast(E|X(x))\) switches to any finite base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S(s)\).

It's enough, according to 5.2.5, to check that the co-specialization arrows for \((f_{(x)},E|X(x))\) are isomorphisms. By transitivity of the co-specialization arrows (5.2.3), it is enough to show that, if \(u:t\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S(s)\) is a specialization, then \(\operatorname{cosp}(u):\mathsf{R}\Gamma(X(x)_t,E|X(x)_t)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X(x)_s,E|X(x)_s)\) is a isomorphism. But, as \(s\) is closed in \(S(s)\), \(X(x)_s\) is strictly local closed point \(x\), \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma(X(x)_s,E|X(x)_s)=E_x\), and \(\operatorname{cosp}(u)\) is an isomorphism, inverse of restriction isomorphism \(E_x\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X(x)_t,E|X(x)_t)\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.2.7

Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) un \(S\)-morphism, \(p:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), \(q:Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) projections, and \(E\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}^+(X)\). It is assumed \(A\) locally constant, \(f\) locally acyclic rel. to \(E\), and \(q\) locally acyclic at any geometric point \(y\) from \(Y\) rel. to any locally constant sheaf near \(y\). So \(p\) is locally acyclic rel. to \(E\).

Is \(x\) a geometric point of \(x\), images \(y\) and \(s\) in \(Y\) and \(S\), \(t\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S(s)\) a specialization, \(f_{(x)}:X(x)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y(y)\) the morphism induced by \(f\). It is a question of showing that the restriction
\[ \tag{*} E_x \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X(x)_t,E|X(x)_t) \]
isomorphism. We can assume \(A\) constant. 5.2.4 and 5.2.5, for any specialization \(z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y(y)\), the specialisation arrow
\[ E_x = \mathsf{R} {f_{(x)}}_\ast (E|X(x))_y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R} {f_{(x)}}_\ast (E|X(x)|_z = \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X(x)_z,E|X(x)_z) \]
is an isomorphism, i.e. \(\mathsf{R}{f_{(x)}}_\ast(E|X(x))\) is constant in value \(E_x\). Therefore, the assumption of acyclicity on \(q\) implies that the restriction
\[ E_x \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R}\Gamma(Y(y)_y,(E_x)|Y(y)_t) = \mathsf{R}\Gamma\left(Y(y)_t,\mathsf{R} {f_{(x)}}_\ast (E|X(x))|Y(y)_t\right) \]
is isomorphism. But this arrow is identified with (*), hence the conclusion.

The hypothesis of 5.2.7 on \(q\) is checked in particular when \(q\) is smooth, and \(A\) is cancelled by an integer \(n\) invertible on \(S\) [2]On the other hand, if the assumptions of 5.2.7 are verified after any basic change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), then it is concluded that \(p\) is universally locally acyclic rel. to \(E\). Of these remarks derive in particular the statement 2.14.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.2.8

Been \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) and \(E\in \operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}^+(X)\)It is assumed that \(f\) is universally locally acyclic rel. to \(E\), that the training of \(\mathsf{R} f_\ast E\) switch to any finite basic change, and as for any morphism \(s'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} s\), where \(s\) is a geometric point of \(S\) and \(s'\) the spectrum of a separably enclosed field, the base change arrow \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma(X_s,E|X_s)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X_{s'},E|X_{s'})\) is isomorphism. \((f,E)\) is cohomologically clean (5.1.3).

Either \(f':X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) the morphism deduced from \(f\) by a basic change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), it is to see that the formation of \(\mathsf{R} f_\ast'(E|X')\) switches to any finite base change. 5.2.5, it is sufficient to show that, for any specialization \(u':t'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'(s')\), the cospecialization arrow \(\operatorname{cosp}(u')\): \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma(X_{t'}',E|X_{t'}') \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X_{s'}',E|X_{s'}')\) is an isomorphism. Be \(s\) (resp. \(t\)) the geometric point image of \(s\) (resp. \(t\)) in \(S\), \(u:t\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S(s)\) the specialisation defined by \(u'\). It is easy to see that one has a commutative square

\[ \xymatrix{ \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X_t,E|X_t) \ar[r]^-{\operatorname{cosp}(u)} \ar[d] & \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X_s,E|X_s) \ar[d] \\ \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X_{t'}',E|X_{t'}') \ar[r]^-{\operatorname{cosp}(u')} & \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X_{s'}',E|X_{s'}') } \]

where the vertical arrows are the basic change arrows. By hypothesis, these are isomorphisms. On the other hand (5.2.4) \(\operatorname{cosp}(u)\) is an isomorphism, so \(\operatorname{cosp}(u')\) is an isomorphism, which completes the demonstration.

Here is an example of application of 5.2.8. Suppose \(A\) canceled by an integer \(n\) invertible on \(S\), \(f\) locally of finite type and universally locally acyclic. Be \(x\) geometric point of \(X\), image \(s\) in \(S\), \(f_{(x)}:X(x)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S(s)\) morphism induced by \(f\). It results from 5.2.6 and 1.9 (by going to the limit) than the assumptions of 5.2.8 are checked by the couple \((f_{(x)},E|X(x))\), which is therefore cohomologically clean.

Suppose \(A\) constant. Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), and \(E\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}^+(X)\) the finished tor-dimensional complex. We will say that \(f\) is highly locally acyclic rel. to \(E\) if, for any geometric point \(x\) of \(X\), image \(s\) in \(S\), any specialization \(t\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S(s)\), and all \(A^\circ\)-module \(M\), the restriction arrow
\[ E_x\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}_A M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X(x)_t,(E|X(x)_t)\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}_A M) \]
I don't know if "locally acyclic" implies "strongly locally acyclic."

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.2.10

Under the assumptions of 5.2.9, suppose \(A\) noetherian of torsion, \(S\) noetherian, and \(f\) strongly locally acyclic rel. to \(E\). So, for any cartesian square diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[d]^-f & X' \ar[d]^-{f'} \ar[l]_-j& X'' \ar[d]^-{f''} \ar[l]_-{j'}\\ S & S' \ar[l]_-i& S'' , \ar[l]_-{i'}} \]

with \(i\) quasi-finished and \(i'\) open immersion, and all \(F\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}^+(S'',A^\circ)\), the canonical arrow

\[ \tag{*} j^\ast E \overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} {f'}^\ast \mathsf{R} i_\ast' F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R} j_\ast' ((j j')^\ast E\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} {f''}^\ast F) \]

is isomorphism.
This statement will be compared with [2].

Demontons 5.2.10. One can easily return to\(S=S'\), strictly local, and \(F\) constructible, concentrated in degree \(0\). One then solves \(F\) to the right by finite sums of bundles of the form \(u_\ast M\), where \(u:t\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a geometric point and \(M\) a \(A^\circ\)-module. The hypothesis implies that (*) is an isomorphism for \(F=u_\ast M\), hence the result.
